title: The Values and Principles of the UK
overview:
British society is founded on fundamental values and principles which all those living in the UK should respect and support. These values are reflected in the responsibilities, rights and privileges of being a British citizen or permanent resident of the UK. They are based on history and traditions and are protected by law, customs and expectations.

The five fundamental principles of British life are democracy, the rule of law, individual liberty, tolerance of those with different faiths and beliefs, and participation in community life. Understanding these values is essential for anyone who wants to become a permanent resident or citizen of the UK. There is no place in British society for extremism or intolerance.

study_sections.heading: The Five Fundamental Principles
study_sections.content:
British society is built on five fundamental principles that everyone living in the UK should respect and support:

1. Democracy
2. The rule of law
3. Individual liberty
4. Tolerance of those with different faiths and beliefs
5. Participation in community life

These values are not just abstract ideas—they are reflected in everyday life, in how people treat each other, how laws are made and enforced, and how communities function. They are based on history and traditions and are protected by law, customs and expectations. There is no place in British society for extremism or intolerance.

study_sections.heading: The Citizenship Pledge
study_sections.content:
As part of the citizenship ceremony, new citizens pledge to uphold these values. The pledge is:

'I will give my loyalty to the United Kingdom and respect its rights and freedoms. I will uphold its democratic values. I will observe its laws faithfully and fulfil my duties and obligations as a British citizen.'

This pledge demonstrates a commitment to British values and to being a responsible member of society. It reflects the importance of these principles in British life and the expectation that all residents will support them.

study_sections.heading: Responsibilities of UK Residents
study_sections.content:
Following from the fundamental principles are responsibilities which are shared by all those living in the UK. If you wish to be a permanent resident or citizen of the UK, you should:

• Respect and obey the law
• Respect the rights of others, including their right to their own opinions
• Treat others with fairness
• Look after yourself and your family
• Look after the area in which you live and the environment

These responsibilities apply to everyone living in the UK, whether they are citizens, permanent residents, or temporary residents. They reflect the expectation that people will contribute positively to society and respect the rights and freedoms of others.

study_sections.heading: Freedoms Offered by the UK
study_sections.content:
In return for accepting these responsibilities, the UK offers important freedoms to all residents:

• Freedom of belief and religion
• Freedom of speech
• Freedom from unfair discrimination
• A right to a fair trial
• A right to join in the election of a government

These freedoms are protected by law and are fundamental to life in the UK. They ensure that everyone can live their life as they choose, express their views, practice their religion (or no religion), and participate in the democratic process. These freedoms come with the responsibility to respect the same freedoms for others.

study_sections.heading: Living the Values in Practice
study_sections.content:
The values and principles of the UK are not just theoretical—they affect everyday life and behaviour. They mean:

• Respecting the law and legal processes
• Accepting democratic decisions even when you disagree with them
• Allowing others to live their lives and hold their beliefs without interference
• Treating people fairly regardless of their background, religion, or opinions
• Contributing to your community and helping to maintain the local environment
• Participating in democratic processes such as voting
• Standing against extremism and intolerance

By living according to these values, residents help create a fair, peaceful, and democratic society where everyone's rights are respected and protected.

key_facts: British society is founded on fundamental values and principles.
The five fundamental principles are: democracy, the rule of law, individual liberty, tolerance of different faiths and beliefs, and participation in community life.
These values are based on history and traditions and are protected by law, customs and expectations.
There is no place in British society for extremism or intolerance.
New citizens pledge to uphold these values in the citizenship ceremony.
The citizenship pledge includes giving loyalty to the UK, upholding democratic values, and observing laws faithfully.
Residents should respect and obey the law.
Residents should respect the rights of others, including their right to their own opinions.
Residents should treat others with fairness.
Residents should look after themselves and their families.
Residents should look after the area in which they live and the environment.
The UK offers freedom of belief and religion.
The UK offers freedom of speech.
The UK offers freedom from unfair discrimination.
The UK offers the right to a fair trial.
The UK offers the right to join in the election of a government.
These responsibilities and freedoms apply to all those living in the UK.
The values are reflected in the responsibilities, rights and privileges of being a British citizen or permanent resident.
memory_hook: Remember the five fundamental principles with 'D-R-I-T-P': Democracy, Rule of law, Individual liberty, Tolerance, Participation. Think of it as a DRIP that waters British society—each drop represents a value that keeps society healthy and growing.

questions.prompt: How many fundamental principles of British life are there?
questions.explanation: There are five fundamental principles: democracy, the rule of law, individual liberty, tolerance of different faiths and beliefs, and participation in community life.
